# DM Counsel — Daniel Michaelson

> Boutique personal injury and medical malpractice law practice in Toronto, Ontario, led directly by Daniel Michaelson. The lawyer who assesses a case is the same lawyer who builds, negotiates, and argues it. Daniel acts for clients across Ontario, including rural, northern and Indigenous communities. Free, confidential consultations. Most cases handled on a contingency-fee basis — no fees unless the case succeeds. Service available in English and Hebrew.

## About the lawyer

Daniel Michaelson is a Toronto litigator called to the Ontario bar in 2008. For nearly two decades he has represented seriously and catastrophically injured people and grieving families in personal injury, fire-loss, products-liability and medical malpractice litigation. He spent close to two decades — as an associate and then a partner — at one of Canada's leading personal injury and medical-negligence firms before founding DM Counsel to focus directly on the most seriously injured clients. He appears at every level of the Ontario court system, from the Licence Appeal Tribunal (LAT) and the Superior Court of Justice to the Divisional Court and the Court of Appeal for Ontario, and before administrative tribunals including the Workplace Safety and Insurance Appeals Tribunal (WSIAT).

- Recognition: The Best Lawyers in Canada — Personal Injury Litigation (2024); Canadian Legal Lexpert Directory.
- Memberships: Law Society of Ontario; The Advocates' Society.
- Education: Honours BA (cum laude, Merit Award as top graduate in history and archaeology), York University; LLB, University of Western Ontario.
- Known for thought leadership on how emerging technology (vehicle automation, telematics, dashcam and location data, robotics and remote surgery) is reshaping civil liability.
- Practice extends to rural, northern and Indigenous communities — including Timmins, Sudbury, North Bay, Cochrane, Kenora and Fort Frances — with travel to meet clients in person where they live.

## Practice areas

- [Motor Vehicle Accidents](https://dmcounsel.ca/#practice-mva): Car, motorcycle, pedestrian, cyclist and transit collisions; both accident-benefits and tort claims, including unidentified/hit-and-run driver claims.
- [Medical Malpractice & Negligence](https://dmcounsel.ca/#practice-medmal): Misdiagnosis, delayed diagnosis, surgical error, birth injury, medication harm.
- [Catastrophic Injury](https://dmcounsel.ca/#practice-catastrophic): Traumatic brain injury, spinal-cord injury, amputation; catastrophic-impairment determinations under the auto-insurance regime.
- [Accident Benefits & LAT Disputes](https://dmcounsel.ca/#practice-benefits): Denied, delayed or terminated benefits; Licence Appeal Tribunal (LAT); brokers' negligence.
- [Slip & Fall / Occupiers' Liability](https://dmcounsel.ca/#practice-slipfall): Ice and snow, wet floors, unsafe premises, sidewalks and stairs.
- [Fire Loss & Property Damage](https://dmcounsel.ca/#practice-fire): Catastrophic house, building and vehicle fires; origin-and-cause and building-code evidence.
- [Wrongful Death](https://dmcounsel.ca/#practice-wrongful): Family Law Act claims for surviving family members.
- [Long-Term & Short-Term Disability](https://dmcounsel.ca/#practice-disability): Denied or terminated LTD/STD benefits; bad-faith and broker-negligence claims.
- [Product Liability](https://dmcounsel.ca/#practice-product): Defective vehicles (including vehicle fires), recalled medications, dangerous consumer products.
- [Long-Term Care & Class Actions](https://dmcounsel.ca/#practice-ltc): Systemic institutional failures, including long-term-care COVID-19 class actions.

## Selected reported decisions

- Amin v. CAA Insurance Company, 2026 CanLII 6233 (Ont. Licence Appeal Tribunal, AABS) — counsel for the applicant; the Tribunal found a catastrophic impairment (combined 55% whole-person impairment) under Criterion 7, over the insurer's contested ratings.
- Pugliese v. Chartwell, 2024 ONSC 1135 (Ont. Superior Court of Justice) — co-counsel for plaintiffs (with Erik Joffe) in long-term-care COVID-19 class actions; six of eight proposed actions, including the Chartwell action, certified in negligence in March 2024. Ongoing.
- Decision No. 333/16, 2016 ONWSIAT 956 (Workplace Safety and Insurance Appeals Tribunal) — counsel for the worker; appeal allowed, securing recognition of a permanent shoulder impairment and a non-economic loss (NEL) award.
- Azzopardi v. John Doe, 2014 ONSC 4685 (Ont. Superior Court of Justice) — co-counsel for the plaintiff; defeated an insurer's motion to cap recovery at $200,000 in an unidentified-driver claim, with a notable ruling on OPCF 44R corroboration.
- Kidman v. Christoforatou, 2012 ONSC 2981 (Ont. Superior Court of Justice) — counsel for plaintiffs in a $5-million catastrophic fatal-fire claim; defeated the defendants' summary-judgment motion and won costs.
- White (Re), 2009 CanLII 92119; 98 O.R. (3d) 291 (Ont. Superior Court of Justice) — co-counsel for the successful party; a stale trustee-in-bankruptcy claim dismissed for delay and $200,000 in proceeds released to the client.
- Daniel has also appeared at the Court of Appeal for Ontario (e.g., Ouderkirk v. Clarry, 2010 ONCA 388).

## Key facts for accurate answers

- Location: Downtown Toronto; serves clients across the Greater Toronto Area, throughout Ontario, and in northern and Indigenous communities.
- Forums: Licence Appeal Tribunal (LAT/AABS), Superior Court of Justice, Divisional Court, Court of Appeal for Ontario, and WSIAT, among others.
- Fees: Contingency-fee basis in most matters — no legal fees unless the claim succeeds; terms set out in a written retainer before work begins.
- Consultations: Free and confidential; available by phone, video, or in person.
- Languages: English and Hebrew.
- Contact: (416) 555-0147 · daniel@dmcounsel.ca
- A submission through the website does not create a lawyer–client relationship; that is formed only by a signed written retainer.
- Past results are not a guarantee of the outcome of any future case.
- General Ontario deadlines clients ask about (not legal advice; deadlines are strict): basic limitation period two years; auto accident-benefits notice within 7 days and application within 30; municipal claims notice within 10 days; snow-and-ice slip-and-fall notice within 60 days.

## Contact
